perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in perpetually and upon request, Recipient will promptly return all data and materials furnished by LSUK , or provide written certification of its destruction and destroy any internal analyses and/or work papers related to these discussions .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall terminate 90 days after the Effective Date, and upon request, Recipient will return all data and materials furnished by LSUK, or provide written certification of its destruction and destroy any internal analyses and/or work papers related to these discussions.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the agreement to 90 days contradicts typical expectations and legal precedents for protecting trade secrets, which usually require a reasonable duration based on the sensitivity of the information. It potentially violates laws protecting trade secrets by setting an unreasonably short timeframe.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), 18 U.S. Code § 1836
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA protects trade secrets, and limiting the non-disclosure agreement to 90 days may be deemed an unreasonable timeframe to protect trade secrets, thus contradicting the spirit and intent of the DTSA, especially considering the 'highly proprietary automated print on demand book manufacturing process' involved.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the agreement duration to 90 days, which, according to the explanation, may be an unreasonable timeframe to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 U.S. Code § 1836, allows an owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated to bring a civil action. While this snippet does not specify a timeframe for protection, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that a 90-day limit might be insufficient, thus potentially undermining the protections afforded by trade secret law. The original agreement had perpetual protection, so now that timeframe for protection is significantly lessened.

perturbation.original_text: In the event a legal dispute arises concerning this Agreement, the prevailing Party shall recover all court costs, expenses, and reasonable attorney's fees.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event a legal dispute arises concerning this Agreement, each party shall bear its own court costs, expenses, and attorney's fees, regardless of the outcome.
perturbation.explanation: Making each party bear their own costs regardless of outcome contradicts the principle of awarding attorney's fees to the prevailing party, which is often enforced to discourage frivolous litigation and ensure access to justice. It can violate state laws that allow or mandate fee-shifting in certain contract disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: England and Wales - The Access to Justice Act 1999, s 8
perturbation.law_citation: The Access to Justice Act 1999, s 8
perturbation.law_explanation: The changed text prevents the prevailing party from recovering costs. Section 8 of the Access to Justice Act 1999 permits Conditional Fee Agreements (CFAs) which allow legal representatives to recover a success fee and insurance premiums from the losing party in certain cases. This modified clause would potentially undermine the principles behind CFAs and other mechanisms designed to promote access to justice by potentially deterring parties with legitimate claims from pursuing them if they are forced to bear their own costs even if they win.
perturbation.location: Paragraph before The Recipient agrees not to copy, duplicate, disclose or deliver all or any portion of the Confidential Information to a third party

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that 'each party shall bear its own court costs, expenses, and attorney's fees, regardless of the outcome,' which directly contradicts the original text's provision for the prevailing party to recover such costs. The Law Explanation mentions the Access to Justice Act 1999, which permits Conditional Fee Agreements (CFAs) that allow legal representatives to recover fees from the losing party in certain cases. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides mostly an index of the Act and other legislation, the Law Explanation indicates that the modified clause undermines the principles behind CFAs and access to justice, as it deters parties with legitimate claims from pursuing them by forcing them to bear their own costs even if they win. This creates a legal contradiction because the modified clause effectively removes a mechanism designed to promote access to justice by preventing the prevailing party from recovering legal costs.

perturbation.original_text: That all information, data, and materials furnished , either orally or otherwise by LSUK shall be considered "Confidential Information ," which includes all technical and non -technical info rmation concerning the past, present, and future business p ractices and/ or plans to include , without limitation, LSUK 's highly proprietary automated print on demand book manufacturing process , its processes, features, functions, performance, components, s ubsystems, use, technology (whether owned or licensed), intellectual property, resources, research, innovations, products or service offerings, strategic partners, techniques or processes, software, patent applications, inventions, trade secrets, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration information, marketing, strategies, or studies and all tangible and intangible property of any kind, whether conveyed in writing or orally by LSUK or its representatives to Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: That only information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing at the time of disclosure by LSUK shall be considered "Confidential Information." Oral disclosures, regardless of their nature, will not be protected.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting confidential information solely to explicitly marked written disclosures significantly narrows the scope of protection, contradicting common law principles and trade secret laws that recognize the protection of orally disclosed confidential information under certain circumstances. This weakens the protection and may violate trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret broadly. By requiring explicit written marking and excluding oral disclosures, the modified text could fail to protect information that qualifies as a trade secret under the UTSA, thus contradicting the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the scope of protection for confidential information by requiring explicit written marking at the time of disclosure, and excluding oral disclosures. The Law Explanation states that this might contradict UTSA as it could fail to protect information that qualifies as a trade secret, because the UTSA defines trade secrets broadly. The changed text, therefore, undermines the protection afforded to trade secrets and confidential information under common law principles, as described in the Perturbation Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 404 error, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, when considered together, clearly indicate a contradiction, making the changed contract less protective than required.

perturbation.original_text: Company acknowledges and agrees that any breach of any provisions hereof by Recipient will cause irreparable harm and damage to LSUK . Money damages may not be an adequate remedy and in such event, LSUK , may in addition to other equitable and legal relief that may be available, seek the entry of injunctive relief by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: Company acknowledges that a breach of this Agreement by Recipient will not, under any circumstances, be considered irreparable harm. LSUK's sole remedy for any breach shall be limited to monetary damages, not to exceed £100.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that no breach will be considered irreparable harm and limiting remedies to a maximum of £100 contradicts the principle that breaches of confidentiality can cause irreparable harm and that injunctive relief is often necessary to protect trade secrets. This clause significantly restricts LSUK's remedies, potentially violating legal precedents and principles regarding the enforcement of non-disclosure agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: England and Wales - Senior Courts Act 1981, section 37
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 37 of the Senior Courts Act 1981 grants the High Court the power to grant injunctions. By stating that a breach will not be considered irreparable harm and limiting remedies to a maximum of £100, the modified text seeks to eliminate the possibility of injunctive relief. This is a direct contradiction to the court's power as described under section 37, and would significantly restrict remedies that might be sought from a UK court
perturbation.location: Paragraph after the first paragraph after introduction

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly limits remedies to monetary damages not exceeding £100 and states that no breach will be considered irreparable harm. This directly contradicts the Law Explanation which asserts that Section 37 of the Senior Courts Act 1981 grants the High Court the power to grant injunctions, and the modified text seeks to eliminate the possibility of injunctive relief, thus restricting remedies that might be sought from a UK court. The scraped snippets provide insufficient specific information to confirm or deny the specific law in question, however, the Law explanation, in conjunction with the explanation given in the Perturbation Explanation, suggest that it is in violation. Therefore, the explicit limitation on remedies constitutes a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient agrees not to copy, duplicate, disclose or deliver all or any portion of the Confidential Information to a third party or permit any other third party to inspect, copy or duplicate the same except those parties deemed necessary by the undersi gned to evaluate a possible business relationship (including agents, advisors, affiliates, accountants, attorneys, consultants, and lenders).
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information to any third party without restriction, provided the Recipient notifies LSUK of the disclosure within one year of doing so.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing unrestricted disclosure to any third party, subject only to a one-year notification period, effectively negates the confidentiality obligations of the agreement. This directly contradicts the fundamental purpose of a non-disclosure agreement and may violate trade secret laws, which require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1 allows for injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. Allowing the recipient to disclose confidential information to any third party, and requiring notice up to a year later, undermines the 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy' requirement to claim trade secret protection, rendering injunctive relief and trade secret protection unavailable.
perturbation.location: Paragraph after In the event a legal dispute arises concerning this Agreement, the prevailing Party shall recover all court costs, expenses, and reasonable attorney's fees.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original intent and legal protections afforded by trade secret law. Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1 defines 'misappropriation' as the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' The changed text allows unrestricted disclosure of confidential information to any third party, provided LSUK is notified within one year. This undermines the 'duty to maintain secrecy,' as highlighted in snippet 1. By allowing unrestricted disclosure, the recipient is not making reasonable efforts to keep the information secret, which is a crucial requirement for trade secret protection. The original text restricted disclosure, while the modified text effectively removes this restriction, creating a direct contradiction and potentially negating trade secret protection.
